system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

done", "timeout": 30}

It uses: - **Next.js 15** + **TypeScript** + **Tailwind 3.4.19** - **Supabase** (PostgreSQL + Auth) - **Cloudflare Pages** (deployment) - **Token-based theming** (CSS variables, not hardcoded colors) - **21 reusable section components** serving 20+ business types ## AI Agent Quick Start ### When Starting Work on This Project 1. **Read This File** - You are here! ✅ 2. **Read CLAUDE.md** - Technical architecture and coding standards 3. **Check Current State** - Run `git status` to see what's happening 4. **Check Open PRs** - Run `gh pr list` to see in-flight work 5. **Review Recent Commits** - Run `git log --oneline -10` ### Critical Rules (NEVER Break These) ``` ⚠️ DO NOT upgrade Tailwind to v4 (breaks build) ⚠️ DO NOT hardcode colors - always use var(--primary) ⚠️ DO NOT skip business_id in database queries ⚠️ DO NOT commit .env files ⚠️ DO NOT silently catch errors - always log/rethrow ``` ## Project Structure for AI Agents ``` src/ # Data layer (JSON configs) ├── schemas/ # Business type schemas ├── tokens/ # Design tokens (colors, fonts) ├── registry/ # Business type registry └── content/ # Content templates web/ # Application layer ├── app/ │ ├── [business]/ # Generated sites (SSG) │ ├── admin/ # Admin dashboard │ ├── api/ # API routes │ └── s/[locale]/ # Multi-tenant sites ├── components/ │ ├── sections/ # 21 reusable sections │ └── ui/ # UI primitives (CVA-based) ├── lib/ │ ├── supabase/ # Database clients │ ├── engine/ # Template engine │ └── types.ts # TypeScript types └── scripts/ # Build/generation scripts ``` ## AI-Specific Patterns ### 1. Adding New Business Type (Most Common Task) When adding a new business type (e.g., `dentist`, `mechanic`): **Required Files:** ``` src/ ├── registry/[type].type.json # Section config, features ├── tokens/[type].tokens.json # Colors, fonts ├── content/[type].content.json # Spanish copy templates └── schemas/[type].schema.json # Validation schema ``` **Reuses:** - ✅ Existing section components from `web/components/sections/` - ✅ Base tokens from `src/tokens/base.tokens.json` - ✅ No new React components needed (usually) **Example (5-minute add):** ```json // src/registry/dentist.type.json { "id": "dentist", "nameEs": "Consultorio Dental", "tokens": "dentist", "sections": ["hero", "services", "team", "testimonials", "contact"], "features": { "booking": true, "emergency": true } } ``` ### 2. Adding New Section Component When adding a new reusable section: **File:** `web/components/sections/[name]-section.tsx` **Pattern:** ```typescript interface [Name]SectionProps { title: string items: Array<{...}> // Theme automatically applied via CSS variables } export function [Name]Section({ title, items }: [Name]SectionProps) { return (

{title}

{/* Content */}
) } ``` **Registration:** Add to `web/lib/engine/renderer.tsx` ### 3. Database Schema Changes When modifying Supabase schema: **Location:** `supabase/migrations/000_complete_schema.sql` **Rules:** - Add new tables at the end - Include RLS policies for every table - Add indexes for query performance - Create views for analytics if needed - Test with `npm run typecheck` ### 4. Environment Variables **Local:** `web/.env.local` (gitignored) **Production:** Cloudflare Pages dashboard **Required:** ``` NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL= NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY= SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY= ``` **Optional:** ``` MERCADOPAGO_PUBLIC_KEY= MERCADOPAGO_ACCESS_TOKEN= NEXT_PUBLIC_GA_MEASUREMENT_ID= ``` ## AI-Optimized Workflows ### Workflow 1: Generate Preview Site for Lead **Context:** Admin wants to generate a demo site for a lead **Steps:** 1. Query lead from `leads` table 2. Compose page using `composePage()` from business type 3. Generate static HTML 4. Store in `generated_sites` table 5. Return preview URL **Key Files:** - `web/lib/engine/compose.ts` - Page composition - `web/lib/engine/renderer.tsx` - Section rendering - `web/app/admin/leads/page.tsx` - Admin UI ### Workflow 2: WhatsApp Outreach **Context:** Send templated WhatsApp message to lead **Steps:** 1. Get lead phone/whatsapp from `leads` table 2. Generate wa.me link with templated message 3. Log event to `outreach_events` table 4. Update lead status to `contacted` **Template Location:** `web/lib/outreach/templates.ts` (create if needed) ### Workflow 3: MercadoPago Payment **Context:** Lead wants to subscribe **Steps:** 1. Create subscription in `subscriptions` table 2. Generate MercadoPago checkout 3. Handle webhook at `/api/webhooks/mercadopago` 4. Update subscription status on payment 5. Grant access to self-service editing ## Common AI Tasks & Solutions ### Task: Fix Build Error **Checklist:** 1. `npm run typecheck` - TypeScript errors 2. `npm run lint` - ESLint errors 3. Check for Tailwind v4 (should be 3.4.19) 4. Check for hardcoded colors (should use CSS vars) 5. Check for missing `business_id` in queries ### Task: Debug Database Connection **Checklist:** 1. `.env.local` exists with correct keys 2. Supabase project is running 3. RLS policies allow the operation 4. Using correct client (server vs browser vs admin) ### Task: Add New Feature **Questions to Ask:** 1. Can this reuse existing sections? (95% of cases: YES) 2. Does it need new database tables? (check schema first) 3. Is it admin-only or public? (affects auth) 4. Does it need MercadoPago? (payments feature) ### Task: Optimize Performance **Strategies:** 1. Use Server Components by default 2. Lazy load heavy components 3. Add database indexes for common queries 4. Use Cloudflare caching rules 5. Minimize client-side JavaScript ## AI Tools Available ### Firecrawl Skills (Web Scraping) Installed and ready to use: ```typescript // Use skill for web research import { skill } from '@/lib/skills' // Example: Research competitor websites await skill('firecrawl-scrape', { url: 'https://competitor.com', format: 'markdown' }) ``` **Available Skills:** - `firecrawl-scrape` - Single page extraction - `firecrawl-crawl` - Site-wide crawling - `firecrawl-search` - Web search with content - `firecrawl-map` - URL discovery - `firecrawl-agent` - Structured data extraction - `firecrawl-download` - Bulk download ### When to Use Skills ✅ Research competitor pricing ✅ Extract content from existing business websites ✅ Find images/logos for leads ✅ Validate business information ❌ Don't use for: Database queries, template rendering, auth operations ## Testing for AI Agents ### Quick Test Commands ```bash # Type checking npm run typecheck # Build test npm run build # Unit tests npm run test:unit # E2E tests (requires dev server) npm run dev & npm run test:e2e ``` ### What to Test **New Business Type:** - Registry file is valid JSON - Tokens file has all required fields - Content template renders correctly - Section order works in compose **New Section:** - Renders without errors - Accepts all props - Uses CSS variables (no hardcoded colors) - Responsive on mobile **Database Changes:** - Migration runs without errors - RLS policies work - Indexes improve query speed ## AI Communication Guidelines ### Commit Messages Use conventional commits: ``` feat: Add dentist business type fix: Resolve token resolution for missing palettes refactor: Simplify hostname mapping docs: Upda [...truncated AGENTS.md: 9,950 chars total] [Subdirectory context discovered: magnolia-peluqueria/AGENTS.md] # This is NOT the Next.js you know This version has breaking changes — APIs, conventions, and file structure may all differ from your training data. {"code": "from hermes_tools import terminal
import json, os

repos = [
    "/root/3md-website", "/root/awesome-opencode", "/root/bdsm-py-toolkit",
    "/root/bichos-gym", "/root/brahm-the-racoon", "/root/clawpanel",
    "/root/client-leads", "/root/clinica-duerksen", "/root/cocodrilo-fitness",
    "/root/dayah-litworks", "/root/dayah-litworks-deploy", "/root/depiflash",
    "/root/elviajero-comercio", "/root/fun4me-store", "/root/hermes-incident-commander",
    "/root/hermes-ui", "/root/hermes-web-ui", "/root/hermes-webui",
    "/root/laura-egg-business", "/root/luis-de-leon-concept",
    "/root/magnolia-peluqueria", "/root/mantra-spa",
    "/root/nicolas-duarte-career", "/root/nudo/repo",
    "/root/ozmontania-website", "/root/paragu-ai-builder",
    "/root/paragu-ai-leads", "/root/paraguay-beauty-data",
    "/root/refugio-animal-paraguay", "/root/solstein",
    "/root/space-agent", "/root/stroopwafel-huis", "/root/vete",
]

results = []
for r in repos:
    name = os.path.basename(r)
    
    # remote URL
    remote = terminal(f"cd '{r}' && git remote get-url origin 2>/dev/null", timeout=5)
    url = remote['output'].strip() if remote['exit_code'] == 0 else "-"
    
    # primary language - check package.json, pyproject.toml, etc.
    lang = "?"
    for f in ["package.json", "pyproject.toml", "Cargo.toml", "go.mod", "Gemfile", "composer.json"]:
        fp = os.path.join(r, f)
        if os.path.exists(fp):
            if f == "package.json":
                lang = "TypeScript/JS (Next.js?)"
            elif f == "pyproject.toml":
                lang = "Python"
            elif f == "Cargo.toml":
                lang = "Rust"
            elif f == "go.mod":
                lang = "Go"
            elif f == "Gemfile":
                lang = "Ruby"
            elif f == "composer.json":
                lang = "PHP"
            break
    
    # check for Docker compose
    has_docker = os.path.exists(os.path.join(r, "docker-compose.yml"))
    has_dockerfile = os.path.exists(os.path.join(r, "Dockerfile"))
    
    # last commit
    last = terminal(f"cd '{r}' && git log --oneline -1 2>/dev/null", timeout=5)
    last_commit = last['output'].strip() if last['exit_code'] == 0 else "-"
    
    # branch
    branch = terminal(f"cd '{r}' && git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null", timeout=5)
    br = branch['output'].strip() if branch['exit_code'] == 0 else "-"
    
    results.append({
        "name": name,
        "path": r,
        "lang": lang,
        "remote": url,
        "branch": br,
        "last_commit": last_commit[:80],
        "docker_compose": has_docker,
        "dockerfile": has_dockerfile,
    })

# Print table
print(f"{'REPO':<40} {'LANG':<22} {'BRANCH':<14} {'DOCKER':<8} {'REMOTE':<45}")
print("="*130)
for r in sorted(results, key=lambda x: x['name'].lower()):
    docker_flag = "Y" if r['docker_compose'] or r['dockerfile'] else ""
    remote_short = r['remote'].replace("[REDACTED_EMAIL]:", "").replace("https://github.com/", "").strip()[:44]
    print(f"{r['name']:<40} {r['lang']:<22} {r['branch']:<14} {docker_flag:<8} {remote_short:<45}")
"}

{"command": "cd /root && for d in \
  3md-website awesome-opencode bdsm-py-toolkit bichos-gym brahm-the-racoon \
  clawpanel client-leads clinica-duerksen cocodrilo-fitness dayah-litworks \
  dayah-litworks-deploy depiflash elviajero-comercio fun4me-store \
  hermes-incident-commander hermes-ui hermes-web-ui hermes-webui \
  laura-egg-business luis-de-leon-concept magnolia-peluqueria mantra-spa \
  nicolas-duarte-career nudo/repo ozmontania-website paragu-ai-builder \
  paragu-ai-leads paraguay-beauty-data refugio-animal-paraguay solstein \
  space-agent stroopwafel-huis vete; do
  n=$(basename "$d")
  lang="?"
  [ -f "$d/package.json" ] && lang="Next.js/TS"
  [ -f "$d/pyproject.toml" ] && lang="Python"
  [ -f "$d/Cargo.toml" ] && lang="Rust"
  url=$(cd "$d" 2>/dev/null && git remote get-url origin 2>/dev/null || echo "-")
  br=$(cd "$d" 2>/dev/null && git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo "-")
  lc=$(cd "$d" 2>/dev/null && git log --oneline -1 2>/dev/null || echo "-")
  dk=""
  [ -f "$d/docker-compose.yml" -o -f "$d/Dockerfile" ] && dk="Y"
  printf "%-36s | %-12s | %-8s | %-3s | %-40s\n" "$n" "$lang" "$br" "$dk" "${url##*github.com/}"
done", "timeout": 30}

Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) → VPS → Traefik → fun4me_web:3000

Home, Categorías/[slug], Producto/[slug], Carrito, Checkout, Confirmación, Buscar, Cuenta, Eventos, Kink/[slug], Ofertas, Envíos, Devoluciones, Privacidad, FAQ, Blog (listing + [slug] + category), Nosotros, Términos, Verificar-edad, Login, Registro, Recuperar contraseña, Admin (dashboard + CRUD: productos, categorías, pedidos, cupones, kinks, anuncios, eventos, lista-negra, verificaciones)", "exit_code": 0, "error": null}

# Client Business Intelligence

Systematic approach to researching a client business, identifying opportunities, and preparing a pitch.

## Workflow

### Phase 1: Surface-Level Research (30 min)

### Source Priority (run in this order, stop when you have enough)

1. **Their current website** — identify all pages, products, about page, press page, contact. Check for `/nosotros/`, `/tiendas/`, `/prensa/`, `/blog/` — these often have buried gold (subsidiaries, partnerships, director names).
2. **Infonegocios / ABC Color / Última Hora** — PY business press. Use curl + sed for article extraction since these sites are usually accessible without JS. Search: `"{company}" director gerente dueño`
3. **LinkedIn** — `/company/{name}` for corporate page. Note: ALWAYS blocked by auth wall. Can confirm page EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry.
4. **Google Maps** — `https://www.google.com/maps/search/{company}+Paraguay/`. Use the browser tool, then extract `[role=feed]` entries for store names, addresses, ratings, phones.
5. **Facebook** — `/pages/{name}` or `/{name}`. Usually accessible, basic info visible.
6. **DuckDuckGo lite** — `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={query}'` — no-JS, no-block, good for PY corporate queries
7. **Bing** — fallback when Google blocks. `/search?q={query}` with a desktop UA usually works.
8. **Corporate registry** — SET RUC (`dnit.gov.py/web/portal-institucional/consulta-ruc?ruc={RUC}`), Mercantil Registry, InfoCiudad. WARNING: These almost always return 404. Try once then move on.

### Search Queries to Try

```bash
# Ownership/directors
"{company} dueños directores familia"
"{company} gerente director"
"{company} nombre del dueño"

# Subsidiaries
"{company} Argentina filial"
"{company} subsidiaria"

# Press articles
"\"{company}\" Infonegocios"
"\"{company}\" ABC Color"
"\"{company}\" Última Hora"

# General when others fail
curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={company}+Paraguay'
```

**For web scraping without getting blocked:**
- Use `curl -sL 'https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...' -H 'User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0'` for search results
- Use `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q=...'` for simple text extraction
- For blocked sites: Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora are good sources for PY corporate news
- LinkedIn auth wall always blocks — note that the profile EXISTS but can't read without login
- Google blocks aggressive IPs — use Bing or DDG as fallback

### Phase 2: Deep Research (1-2 hours)

1. **Read press articles** — search Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora for interviews with directors
2. **Extract key quotes** — director strategy quotes are gold for understanding priorities
3. **Map corporate structure** — parent, subsidiaries, strategic partners, major clients
4. **Product family analysis** — every product line, cross-sell pairs, pricing tiers, warranty differences
5. **Competitive landscape** — list direct competitors, their focus, Superspuma's advantages

### Phase 3: Opportunity Analysis

1. **Cross-sell math** — calculate the upsell potential per customer (e.g., mattress + base + protector + almohada = +Gs. 1.17M)
2. **B2B opportunities** — wholesale client portals, hotel/hospitality programs, government contracts
3. **Market gaps** — what competitors don't do that this client could
4. **Geographic expansion** — Argentina subsidiary (if exists), Brazil border cities, Mercosur advantages

### Phase 4: Contact Documentation

1. **Research the contact** — LinkedIn, news mentions, Google
2. **Document what's known vs unknown** — be honest about gaps
3. **Map authority level** — can they make decisions? Who else needs to be involved?
4. **Build relationship strategy** — warm intro? Cold outreach? Existing relationship?

### Phase 5: Pitch Preparation

Create `contacts/deal-strategy.md` with:
- Deal options (tiered from low to high scope)
- Step-by-step conversation flow (open with value → show → ask → present opportunity → close)
- Talking points (what to highlight, what NOT to say)
- Objection handling for likely concerns
- Competitive positioning (why us vs agencies)
- Pricing signals (monthly retainer, project-based, revenue share)

## File Structure for Client Documentation

```
{client-repo}/
├── contacts/
│   ├── README.md              # Index of all contacts & documents
│   ├── {contact-name}.md       # Profile + relationship strategy
│   └── deal-strategy.md        # Pitch guide + deal options
├── docs/
│   ├── business-analysis.md    # Corporate research
│   └── opportunity-analysis.md # Deal opportunities
```

### Service-at-Home Safety Protocol

When the client offers a **mobile/at-home service** (depilación a domicilio, delivery, etc.), proactively build a safety protocol addressing:

**Risk:** "Alguien pide, voy y me roban."

**Mitigation layers:**
1. **Pre-verification (call, not just text)** — Llmar antes de ir. Si no contesta, no vas.
2. **Location sharing** — Compartir ubicación en vivo con contacto de confianza.
3. **Check-in/out protocol** — "Voy a [dirección]" → "Entré" → "Salí bien". Timer de 30 min.
4. **Intuition rule** — Si algo se siente mal al llegar, cancelás. Decís "perdón, me surgió un problema".
5. **First-session filter** — Ofrecer primera sesión gratis/descuento profundo a las primeras X personas. Sirve como filtro y genera confianza mutua.
6. **No carry cash** — Preferir transferencia bancaria. Menos incentivo al robo.

Documentar en `docs/safety-protocol.md` en el repo del cliente. Incluir checklist diario imprimible y plan de emergencia (qué hacer si pasa algo). Ver `references/service-at-home-safety.md` para template completo.

---

### Phase 6: Service Expansion Research (new service line for existing client)

When a client with an existing business wants to add a NEW service line targeting an underserved segment (e.g., affirmative depilation for trans women, men's grooming, post-op care):

**Step 1: 20-Question Structured Questionnaire with Multiple-Choice Options**

Format: Markdown with 4 blocks of 5 questions each (20 total). Each question has 4-5 pre-generated options (a/b/c/d/e). The MC format forces the client to decide fast instead of rambling.

**Important: include blank "Otro: ________" lines** — clients often combine or modify options. Document their exact words, not the closest option.

Blocks are always:
- Block 1 (Q1-4): Validación personal / Motivación
- Block 2 (Q5-8): Equipo / Técnica
- Block 3 (Q9-12): Precios / Modelo de negocio
- Block 4 (Q13-16): Marketing / Contacto comunitario
- Block 5 (Q17-20): Seguridad / Privacidad / Riesgos

After the client responds, **make the remaining decisions as IA** — don't send the parsed answers back for approval. The client said "decide todo como IA". This includes: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy variations. Document the reasoning for each decision.

If the client leaves a question open ("investiga online y decide"), research and decide. Don't ask again.

2. **Análisis de respuestas** — Las respuestas del dueño revelan prioridades, presupuesto, conocimientos, y miedos. Documentar textualmente, incluyendo correcciones y anotaciones que el dueño agrega a las opciones pre-generadas. Extract all "decide todo como IA" signals and act on them.

3. **Research en 7 lotes paralelos** — Dividir los 100+ temas en lotes: A) Mercado/competencia, B) Máquina/equipo, C) Comunidad, D) Médico, E) Marketing, F) Finanzas, G) Psicología, H) Logística. Investigar cada lote y compilar en un documento master.

4. **Competitor pricing via browser scraping** — Para clínicas/competidores PY:
   - Abrir con `browser_navigate` los sitios de competidores directos
   - Usar `browser_snapshot(full=true)` para capturar precios, zonas, packs
   - Extraer precios reales visibles en el HTML (ej: DepilArte tenía Gs. 49,000-220,000 visibles)
   - Si el sitio está bloqueado, probar `curl -sL` con User-Agent desktop o DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...`)
   - Para sitios sin precios visibles, estimar basado en: tamaño de zona (mini/pink/pequeña/mediana/especial/grande), y precio promedio de mercado PY

5. **Search engine evasion** — Google, Bing, Brave, and Ecosia all block VPS IPs after 1-2 requests. DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`html.duckduckgo.com/html`) works but also blocks after ~3 requests with a CAPTCHA. Recovery strategies:
   - Send requests with rotated User-Agent headers via `curl`
   - Use Browserbase (when available) which routes through residential proxies
   - Fall back to training data knowledge for common business intelligence topics (Paraguay market, IPL pricing, trans community orgs)
   - Accept blocked results as "not accessible via automation" rather than retrying endlessly

6. **Master document compilation** — Después de investigar todos los lotes, compilar en `docs/trans-research-master.md` (o nombre análogo) con:
   - Cada lote como sección A-H
   - Una tabla de resumen ejecutivo al final con decisiones clave (precio, descuento, marketing, plan)
   - Subir a repo y dar link al dueño

7. **100-item research checklist** — After the master document, generate a separate file `docs/{topic}-research-100-list.md` with 100 specific questions/items organized in the same categories. This serves as: progress tracker, idea source for next iterations, and documentation of research scope. Each item is 1 sentence.

8. **Website audit for existing clients** — When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:
   - **Structure map** — every page, component, and content file
   - **Content audit** — es.json (or equivalent): find placeholders ("****"), duplicates (two FAQ sections), missing sections (no mention of new service)
   - **UX/Design** — photos vs emoji, social proof, about section, favicon
   - **SEO** — sitemap, robots, og:image, meta tags, schema
   - **Performance** — DOM size, load time, cache headers
   - **Score each category** 1-10 with justification
   - **Prioritized improvement plan** — 3 tiers: 🔴 esta semana, 🟡 este mes, 🟢 cuando haya tiempo. Each item has: effort estimate, impact assessment, specific files to change.

9. **Action plan from audit** — After the audit:
   - Decide all pricing/package decisions as IA
   - Generate a 7-day checklist (día por día) 
   - Generate a 30-day plan with milestones
   - Generate WhatsApp messages ready to send (4 templates: new client, confirmation, 24h follow-up, 20-day reminder)
   - Push all docs to repo, give client the GitHub link

## Pitfalls

- **LinkedIn always requires login** — accept that you'll only see the auth wall. Note the profile EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry multiple times.
- **Instagram handle ≠ correct name** — e.g., an artist's studio has IG @ocho.estudio but the correct name is "Estudio 8", not "Ocho Estudio". Always verify the actual name from the artist's own materials (interviews quotes, personal bio), not from the Instagram handle or press articles that copy the handle.
- **Press articles may use wrong name** — journalists often copy the Instagram handle without verifying. An article might say "Ocho Estudio" when the real name is "Estudio 8". Preserve the press's wording in article excerpts (they wrote that, not us) but use the correct name in your own site content (bio, timeline, nav).
- **City list vs country count** — press articles often give explicit city lists that contradict the site's broad claim (e.g., site says "10 countries" but press mentions 21+ specific cities). When cross-referencing, prefer the article's granular data. The artist's actual reach is usually understated in a terse site stat.
- **Cross-reference built site against research** — when an existing site is already built, don't just research in isolation. Produce a gap analysis: "what's in the built site vs what research reveals." This includes: works present but lacking images, works missing entirely, scale understated, press underutilized. The deliverable is a structured comparison, not a blank-slate recommendation.
- **Instagram blocks all automated access** — `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE` always. Don't waste time retrying Instagram directly. See `references/instagram-osint.md` for workarounds.
- **Google blocks aggressively** — after 2-3 searches from the same IP, you get a CAPTCHA wall. Switch to DDG lite or Bing immediately.
- **PY corporate registries are unreliable** — SET (DNIT), Mercantil, InfoCiudad often return 404. News articles are more reliable than official registries. Always try once but don't spend more than 30 seconds.
- **Paraguay business owners often have low digital footprint** — many executives have no LinkedIn, no social media. "No public results found" is a valid finding, not a failed search.
- **Warm leads are better than cold research** — if the user has a direct contact (like Leticia Roig as GM of Superspuma), prioritize documenting what they know over searching publicly.
- **Don't rely on browser for everything** — Curl with simple User-Agent headers often works where the headless browser gets blocked. Try `curl -sL` before opening the browser for each source.
- **Infonegocios articles are gold** — they often have director interviews with strategy quotes, company ambitions, competitive claims. Extract the full article text, not just snippets.
- **See `references/search-engine-evasion-pattern.md`** for the full ordered fallback chain when Google/DDG/Bing/Brave all block automated queries. Yahoo is the most reliable VPS-search-engine as of 2026-05. Wikipedia API always works. Instagram data can be extracted from Yahoo search result snippets.

### Pitfall: Template/boilerplate leftovers

When the client site was generated from a multi-tenant builder like `paragu-ai-builder`, check for **stale template files** in `lib/client-kit/` (or equivalent). These are the generic versions of the actual components in `components/`. They are NOT used by the live site but confuse future edits. Recommend cleaning them up in a follow-up commit.

**Detection:** `find . -not -path './node_modules/*' -not -path './.git/*' -not -path './.next/*' -type f | sort` shows component files in TWO locations (e.g. `components/header.tsx` + `lib/client-kit/header.tsx`). The `components/` versions are the live ones.

## Website Audit (for existing clients)

When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:

### Structure map
- Every page, component, and content file. Output: tree of `app/`, `components/`, `content/`.

### Content audit
- Check `es.json` (or equivalent): find **placeholders** (`"****"`, `"placeholder": true`), **duplicates** (two FAQ arrays), **missing sections** (no mention of new service line), **stale references**.
- Check every page — does it load? Does it have content?

### UX/Design scoring
- Score each category 1-10: Funcionalidad, Diseño, Contenido, Prueba Social, SEO, Performance, UX Móvil
- Justify each score in 1 sentence
- Identify the biggest gap (typically "Contenido" or "Prueba Social")

### SEO checklist
- Favicon? (link rel="icon" in head)
- og:image for social shares?
- Sitemap.xml? robots.txt?
- Canonical URL?
- JSON-LD schema?

### Performance
- Load time (from browser performance API)
- DOM size
- Cache headers
- HTTP protocol (h3 is good)

### Prioritized improvement plan
3 tiers with effort estimate and impact:
- 🔴 **Critical** (hacer esta semana) — e.g., favicon, placeholder fix, new service section, Instagram link, photo of owner
- 🟡 **Medium** (hacer este mes) — e.g., real photos, og:image, unified FAQs, social proof counters
- 🟢 **Low** (cuando haya tiempo) — e.g., blog, dark mode, animations, chatbot

Each item should specify the file(s) to change and the exact line/region.

### Implementation after audit

When the client says "implementa todos" or "decide todo como IA":
1. Apply ALL changes identified in the audit
2. For pricing/service decisions left open by the client: research, decide, document reasoning
3. Build: `npm run build` (or equivalent) and fix any errors
4. Git commit + push
5. Docker build + deploy
6. If deploy fails (resources), note it and tell the client what manual step is needed

### Visual Artist Research

When researching a visual artist (muralist, painter, illustrator) for a website project, use `references/artist-visual-research-pattern.md` for the full ordered workflow. Key differences from business research:

- **No corporate registry** — no RUC, no SET. Use Wikidata + Wikipedia API + news sites.
- **Press articles are the richest source** — interviews reveal style, influences, quotes, career timeline.
- **YouTube is underutilized** — extraction of `ytInitialData` from HTML gives video interviews that news articles don't cover.
- **Name confusion is common** — verify with 2+ sources before concluding. Paraguayan artists may have 1-2 press articles but no Wikipedia presence.
- **Website plan is the deliverable** — unlike business research (deal strategy), artist research produces a complete website architecture (10 pages minimum) with content specification per page, tech stack, monetization strategy, and roadmap.

## Per-session reference file management

After each major research session for a client:
1. Create `references/{client}-{topic}-{date}.md` with condensed findings
2. This file is the **session transcript summary** — not a substitute for the full docs in the client repo
3. Keep it focused on: key findings, techniques learned, decisions that future sessions should know
4. Delete the oldest reference when the list gets too long (5+ files) — the repo docs are the source of truth

## `decide-todo-como-IA` pattern

When the client responds to the 20-question questionnaire and leaves decisions open with "decide todo como IA" or "investiga online y decide":
- Research each open question (competitor pricing, technical specs, community contacts)
- Take a firm decision — don't propose options back
- Document the reasoning for each decision in markdown
- Include a table: "Decisiones de la IA basadas en investigación"
- This applies to: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy

## Verification

After creating the documentation files:
1. Push to repo: `git add contacts/ docs/ && git commit && git push`
2. Verify the site content reflects the research (update es.json with new company info)
3. If adding stores with GPS, add Google Maps links to the store locator page
4. Cross-sell opportunities should be reflected in product page design (show bundles)

# Ecommerce Product Roadmap — Revenue-First, Validation-Driven

Build a roadmap for an ecommerce store. Start from business research, but ALWAYS validate demand before committing to features.

## ⚠️ CRITICAL: The Self-Roast Step (Added v2.0)

After drafting the initial roadmap, ALWAYS run a brutal critique pass:

### Scope Creep Checklist
- Count total features. If >100 for a small business, you've over-scoped.
- Count database tables. If >15 at launch, you're over-engineering.
- For each feature ask: "Does this make money or save time? If not, cut it."
- For each custom-built feature ask: "Can we use an existing tool instead?"
- Calculate total dev hours. If >500h for MVP→V1, scope is too large.
- Check: does every phase generate or protect revenue? If a phase exists purely for "growth" or "community" with zero revenue, restructure it.

### Buy Don't Build (default to existing tools)
| Don't Build            | Use Instead                 | Savings   |
|------------------------|-----------------------------|-----------|
| Forum/community        | Telegram/Discord group      | ~80-120h  |
| Private messaging      | WhatsApp/Telegram DMs       | ~30h      |
| Video platform         | YouTube embeds              | ~20-40h   |
| Complex event system   | Google Forms + simple page  | ~60h      |
| AI chatbot             | WhatsApp human support      | ~40h      |
| Full CRM               | Supabase queries + sheets   | ~30h      |
| Email campaign builder | Resend/Brevo dashboard      | ~20h      |

### Phase Gates (mandatory checkpoints)
Every phase MUST have measurable exit criteria. Do NOT start Phase N+1 until Phase N metrics are met. Example gates:
- Phase 1: "Are we getting orders?"
- Phase 2: "Are card payments improving conversion?"
- Phase 3: "Are customers coming back?"
- Phase 4: "Does content/events drive revenue?"
- Phase 5: "Scale what works. Kill what doesn't."

### Validation Before Building
These features require PROOF OF DEMAND before building:
- Subscription boxes → survey 20+ customers on willingness + price
- Community forum → is there an active Telegram/Discord group first?
- Events/workshops → did free events attract 10+ attendees?
- Loyalty tiers → do you have 500+ customers to warrant tiers?
- Ambassador program → do organic referrals already happen?

### Reality Check Questions
- What's the GDP per capita of the target market?
- What's the total addressable market (TAM) for this niche in this city?
- How many customers does the business currently have?
- What's the monthly revenue baseline?
- Who writes the content? Who moderates? Who fulfills? (operations cost)
- Can 1 developer maintain everything proposed?

## When to Use
- Client has a physical store and wants to go online
- Building an ecommerce site for a specific market/niche
- Need to research payment gateways, shipping, and legal for a specific country
- Creating a phased product plan with effort estimates

## Phase 0: Validation (BEFORE any development)

> This phase was missing in v1 and caused a 239-feature fantasy roadmap. Never skip it.

### Customer Validation
- Interview 15-20 real customers (in-store, WhatsApp, or phone)
- Ask: "Would you buy from a website or do you prefer WhatsApp?"
- Ask: "What products do you wish we carried?"
- Ask about willingness to pay for subscriptions, events, memberships
- Document: current customer count, monthly revenue, AOV, top 20 products

### Legal/Payment Blockers — START EARLY
- Apply for payment gateway merchant account NOW (takes 2-6 weeks in LATAM)
- Verify the payment processor accepts the merchant's product niche
- Register domain, set up DNS/SSL
- Check if any licenses/registrations are needed (e.g., DINAVISA for health products in Paraguay)

### Inventory Audit
- Count actual SKUs (drives schema complexity — 50 products ≠ 5,000 products)
- Determine if products have variants (many niches don't need a variants table)
- Photograph products (this bottlenecks everything)

### Competitor Shopping
- Actually BUY from 1-2 competitors. Document the full experience: ordering, payment, packaging, delivery, communication. This reveals more than any website analysis.

## Phase 1: Business Intelligence (Parallel Research)

Use `delegate_task` with 3 parallel subagents:

### Subagent 1: Business & Social Media Research
- Google Maps listing (address, phone, hours, rating, photos)
- Instagram profile (handle, follower count, content style, posting frequency)
- Facebook page (if exists)
- TikTok presence
- Website (if exists — analyze tech stack, features, UX)
- Brand identity: colors, tone, target audience
- Product catalog: categories, price ranges, bestsellers

### Subagent 2: Market & Competitor Research
- Top competitors in the target country
- What platforms competitors use (Shopify, WooCommerce, custom, Instagram-only)
- Payment gateways available in the country
- Shipping/delivery services in the target city
- Legal considerations for the product niche
- Best practices for the niche (e.g., age verification for adult, cold chain for food)

### Subagent 3: Tech Stack Research
- Recommended stack based on team's existing skills
- Payment integration options with APIs/SDKs for the country
- Inventory management approach
- Order management workflow
- Notification channels (WhatsApp, email, SMS — varies by country)
- Hosting considerations (platform restrictions for certain niches)
- Analytics options

## Phase 2: Synthesize Findings

Create a structured document with:

### Business Profile
| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Name | ... |
| Address | ... |
| Phone | ... |
| Social Media | ... |
| Current Online Presence | ... |
| Rating | ... |

### Market Context
- Market size/fragmentation
- Key competitors with their strengths
- Competitive advantages to build
- Primary marketing channels available

### Tech Stack Decision
- Core framework + database
- Payment processors (primary, secondary, tertiary)
- Notification channels
- Hosting approach
- Why each choice was made

## Phase 3: Database Schema

Design core tables:
- `categories`, `products`, `product_variants`
- `customers` (extends auth), `addresses`
- `cart`, `cart_items`
- `orders`, `order_items`
- `inventory`, `inventory_movements`
- `shipping_zones`
- `coupons`
- `reviews`

Include order status flow diagram.

## Phase 4: Phased Roadmap

### Structure: 6 phases with effort estimates

**Phase 0: Foundation (Week 1)**
- Project init, DB schema, CI/CD, deployment pipeline
- Exit criteria: dev runs, DB has tables, CI passes, deploys work

**Phase 1: MVP — Browse & Buy (Weeks 2-4)**
- Storefront: homepage, categories, product pages, search
- Cart & checkout: guest checkout, address form, basic payment (transfer + COD)
- Basic admin panel: product CRUD, order management
- Exit criteria: customer can browse → add to cart → checkout → pay via transfer/COD

**Phase 2: Online Payments (Weeks 5-6)**
- Primary payment gateway integration (sandbox → production)
- Webhook handlers, auto-confirmation, installments if applicable
- Exit criteria: card payments work end-to-end

**Phase 3: Notifications & Accounts (Weeks 7-8)**
- WhatsApp/SMS notifications (order status changes)
- Customer accounts, order history, saved addresses
- Email notifications
- Exit criteria: automated notifications at each order stage

**Phase 4: Inventory & Operations (Weeks 9-10)**
- Stock tracking, auto-decrement, low-stock alerts
- Bulk import, shipping labels, packing slips
- Exit criteria: no overselling, operational efficiency

**Phase 5: Growth Features — V1 (Weeks 11-14)**
- Coupons/promotions, bundles, free shipping threshold
- Blog/SEO, structured data, sitemap
- Reviews/ratings system
- Wishlist, newsletter
- Analytics dashboard
- Exit criteria: full ecommerce operation

### Each feature entry includes:
- Feature name
- Priority (P0/P1/P2)
- Effort estimate (hours)

## Phase 5: Risks & Legal

Include a risk table:
| Risk | Impact | Mitigation |
|------|--------|------------|

Cover:
- Platform content policy restrictions (hosting, payments)
- Payment processor restrictions for niche
- Legal/regulatory requirements
- Marketing channel restrictions

## Phase 6: Push to Repo

Create `ROADMAP.md` in the repo root with:
1. Business intelligence section
2. Tech stack table
3. Database schema (text diagram)
4. Phased roadmap with all features
5. Post-V1 backlog
6. Legal/compliance checklist
7. Risk table
8. Development principles
9. Timeline summary (visual)

## Phase 7: The Self-Roast (MANDATORY)

After completing the roadmap draft, apply the critique from the top of this document:
1. Run the Scope Creep Checklist
2. Apply Buy Don't Build for every custom feature
3. Add Phase Gates to every phase
4. Mark features that need validation before building
5. Run Reality Check Questions against the target market
6. Cut anything that doesn't make money or save time
7. Verify 1 developer can maintain the final scope

This step typically cuts 40-60% of features and saves hundreds of dev hours.
The first draft is ALWAYS over-scoped — that's normal. The roast fixes it.

## Key Lessons Learned

### Scope Control (Added v2.0 — learned the hard way)
- First drafts inspired by global competitors (Lovehoney, Stockroom) always over-scope
- A boutique in a developing market ≠ a $400M global ecommerce company
- 12 database tables at launch, not 35. Add tables when you need them.
- Community platforms are separate businesses, not features of a store
- Subscription boxes, loyalty tiers, and ambassador programs need validation — not assumption
- Every feature has an operations cost (who writes content? who moderates? who fulfills?)
- WhatsApp-first in LATAM: the website supplements WhatsApp, not the other way around

### Country-Specific Research is Critical
- Payment gateways vary wildly by country (Bancard for Paraguay, Mercado Pago for Argentina, etc.)
- Cash on delivery is essential in cash-heavy economies (Paraguay, much of LATAM)
- WhatsApp is the primary notification channel in LATAM, not email
- Mobile-first is mandatory in developing markets (80%+ mobile traffic)

### Niche-Specific Considerations
- Adult stores: age verification, discreet packaging/billing, hosting restrictions, payment processor restrictions
- Food/perishables: cold chain, expiration tracking, same-day delivery
- Luxury goods: authentication, insurance, premium packaging

### Platform Restrictions to Check Early
- Vercel/Netlify may restrict adult content → self-host
- Shopify restricts adult products → use custom stack
- MercadoPago may reject adult merchants → verify before building integration
- Meta restricts adult product advertising → rely on SEO + WhatsApp marketing
- WhatsApp templates must be discreet for adult stores (no product names)

### Always Research Google Maps First
- Often has the most accurate business info (address, phone, hours, rating)
- Shows the business's linked website/social media
- Reviews give insight into customer expectations
- Works even when Instagram/Facebook require login to view

### Competitor Research — Go Global Then Local
- Use delegate_task with 3 parallel subagents for research (global leaders, LATAM market, tech stack)
- Analyze 8-10 global competitors via browser (not just web search — many adult/niche sites block bots)
- Document killer features per competitor in a structured table
- Create an "Innovations to Steal" list with priority ratings
- BUT THEN: filter through the Reality Check — a Paraguayan boutique doesn't need Lovehoney's feature set

### The Right Database Size by Business Scale
- <100 SKUs, <500 customers: 10-15 tables. No variants table unless needed.
- 100-1000 SKUs, 500-5000 customers: 15-25 tables. Add variants, loyalty, reviews.
- 1000+ SKUs, 5000+ customers: 25+ tables. Now you need the full schema.
- NEVER deploy 35+ tables for a business that hasn't proven online demand yet.

### Deploying Behind Traefik on Shared VPS
When adding a new service to a VPS already running Traefik as a Swarm service on an overlay network (e.g., `agent-net`):
1. Create a NEW bridge network for the service (e.g., `docker network create fun4me-net`)
2. In docker-compose.yml, use that network as `external: true` and set `traefik.docker.network` label to it
3. After `docker compose up -d`, connect Traefik to the new network: `docker network connect fun4me-net $(docker ps -q -f name=traefik_traefik)`
4. This connection is ephemeral — needs re-run after Traefik restart
5. Use the cert resolver name from the existing traefik.yaml (check it — often `letsencryptresolver`, not `letsencrypt`)

### Pitfall: "Build It And They Will Come"
The most dangerous assumption is that a great website = customers. In small LATAM markets:
- Most customers discover via Instagram/WhatsApp, not Google
- The website is a catalog/checkout tool, not a discovery engine (at first)
- SEO takes 3-6 months to generate meaningful traffic
- Marketing budget matters more than features in year 1

## Phase 7.5: Deploy a "Próximamente" Landing Page Immediately

During Phase 0, while waiting for owner inputs (inventory, brand assets, credentials), deploy a real "Coming Soon" page on the actual domain/subdomain. This:
- Gives the client something tangible to show THEIR clients
- Proves the infra works (Docker, Traefik, SSL, CI/CD)
- Unblocks the project psychologically — "it's real, it's live"
- Tests the full deploy pipeline before Phase 1 code hits it

### Steps
1. Scaffold the Next.js project with a single placeholder page
2. Include: store name, "Próximamente", WhatsApp CTA button, contact info
3. Build Docker image, deploy behind reverse proxy, verify SSL
4. Share the live URL with the client immediately
5. Use a temporary subdomain if the real domain isn't ready (e.g., `fun4me.sunstein.cloud`)

### Supabase Credential Handoff
When a client provides Supabase credentials, expect the new key format:
- **Publishable key**: `sb_publishable_...` (replaces old `eyJ...` anon key)
- **Secret key**: `sb_secret_...` (replaces old `eyJ...` service_role key)
- To run schema migrations, you need EITHER: database password, Supabase access token (for CLI `supabase link`), or have the client run SQL manually in the dashboard
- The secret key gives full REST API access but NOT direct SQL execution — you still need the CLI or direct DB connection for migrations
- Always ask for the access token (dashboard → avatar → Access Tokens) alongside the API keys to avoid a second round-trip

### Don't Use Placeholder Domains
If the client's domain isn't decided yet, use YOUR infrastructure subdomain. Clients respond to live URLs, not GitHub repos. A working `https://storename.yourdomain.com` page with a WhatsApp button is worth more than a 50-page roadmap document.

### Create Sample Inventory Alongside
Don't wait for the real inventory. Create 50+ realistic sample products with:
- Proper category taxonomy for the niche
- Local currency pricing at realistic price points
- Experience/difficulty/tier labels if applicable
- Placeholder images or category-appropriate stock images
This lets development proceed on catalog, cart, and checkout while the real inventory is being collected.

## Phase 8: Create GitHub Issues for Agentic Execution

After the roadmap is finalized, create a full issue board so the project can be executed autonomously by AI agents with zero human input (beyond credentials).

### Issue Structure
- Create GitHub labels: `epic`, `story`, `chore`, `blocker`, `owner-action`, `agentic`, `needs-credentials`, `phase-N`, `must-have`
- Create a milestone per phase with due dates
- Create EPICs as parent tracking issues with checklists
- Create stories under each epic with: user story format, tasks, acceptance criteria, dependencies, "needs from owner" section

### Epic Categories for Phase 0
1. **Business Validation** (owner-action) — customer interviews, inventory audit, financial baseline
2. **Legal & Payment Gateway** (owner-action) — Bancard/payment application, RUC, return policy, billing name
3. **Brand Assets** (owner → agentic) — logo/colors from owner, then design system built by agent
4. **Infrastructure & DevOps** (agentic, needs-credentials) — domain, VPS, Coolify, CI/CD, Docker
5. **Project Scaffold** (fully agentic) — Next.js init, testing setup, seed script, README
6. **Communications** (needs-credentials) — WhatsApp Business API, email (Resend)
7. **Shipping & Operations** (owner-action) — zones, prices, delivery methods, packaging

### The "Master Blockers" Issue
Create a single pinned issue titled "📋 MASTER: Everything I Need From [Owner]" that consolidates ALL inputs needed from the business owner into one checklist, categorized by priority:
- 🔴 CRITICAL (blocks all dev): domain, VPS access, Supabase credentials
- 🟡 IMPORTANT (blocks specific features): brand assets, inventory spreadsheet, product photos, Meta Business access, bank details
- 🟢 OWNER ACTIONS (no credentials needed): Bancard application, customer interviews, financial baseline, shipping decisions

Include a "quick-start format" — a template message the owner can copy-paste and fill in to unblock everything at once.

### The Agentic Execution Plan Issue
Create an issue documenting the parallel execution tracks:
- Track A (no deps): project scaffold, CI, testing, README
- Track B (needs domain+VPS): DNS, Coolify, Docker
- Track C (needs Supabase): schema, RLS, seed data
- Track D (needs Meta Business): WhatsApp API
- Track E (needs domain): email setup
- Sequential: brand assets → design system

Include the timeline: "Once credentials received → Phase 0 complete in ~24 hours → Phase 1 MVP starts immediately"

### When Client Can't Provide Anything Yet
Often the client has NO inventory, NO brand assets, NO payment setup, NO financial data. Don't wait. Instead:
1. Close all owner-action issues as "deferred — using sample data"
2. Build the FULL MVP with sample inventory (50+ realistic products)
3. Use AI-designed brand theme (pink/purple for adult, etc.)
4. Use placeholder colored gradients instead of product photos
5. Wire up real Supabase with sample data so the site ACTUALLY WORKS
6. Deploy it live so clients can browse the real experience
7. The client approves the site FIRST, then provides their real data to swap in

This approach is 10x more effective than waiting for assets. Clients respond to working demos, not documents. The conversation goes from "here's a roadmap" to "here's YOUR store, live, with 54 products — what do you want to change?"

### Supabase New API Key Format (2025+)
Supabase now issues two key formats:
- **New format**: `sb_publishable_...` (anon) and `sb_secret_...` (service role)
- **Legacy format**: `eyJ...` JWT tokens (still work, shown as "Legacy" in dashboard)
- Both formats coexist. The legacy JWT keys are needed for `@supabase/supabase-js` client.
- When a client shares credentials, they may paste BOTH formats mixed together. Parse carefully.
- The `sb_publishable` key returns 401 on the REST API schema endpoint — use the legacy anon JWT instead.
- For database migrations: `psql` with the DB password is the most reliable method. The Supabase CLI requires an access token (separate from API keys) which is yet another credential to request.

### Story Template Best Practices
Each story should include:
1. **User story format**: As [role], I need [thing] so that [benefit]
2. **Detailed tasks** with checkboxes (agent can work through them)
3. **Acceptance criteria** (how to know it's done)
4. **"Needs from owner"** section (explicitly what's blocked)
5. **For interview/survey stories**: include the actual script/questions to ask (the owner shouldn't have to figure out what to ask)
6. **For data collection stories**: include a template spreadsheet with exact columns needed
7. **For policy stories**: include a draft for the owner to review (don't make them write from scratch)

URLs fixed. Now let me do the deep research — global kink community analysis, competitor research, and current codebase audit — in parallel.

function.name: delegate_task
function.arguments:
{"goal": "Research the global kink/BDSM/fetish ecommerce and community landscape in depth. Find:
1. Top 10 global kink ecommerce sites (like Stockroom, Lovehoney, Mr S Leather, Subspace, Etsy kink shops) — their revenue model, traffic, feature set, what's special about each
2. Top 5 event/ticketing platforms used by kink community (FetLife events, Eventbrite, specialized)
3. Community platforms (FetLife, Recon, KinkD, etc.) — membership models, revenue, growth
4. Monetization models across the industry: ecommerce + events + subscriptions + memberships + education
5. What's the market size? LATAM-specific opportunities?
6. Payment processor challenges for adult/kink — which ones work, which block this niche
7. Compare paraguay-specific: kink community size, existing players, meetups, clubs
8. Find the most innovative kink-community business models globally (education platforms, subscription boxes, event marketplaces, dungeon rentals, gear rentals)
9. Research specific modern players: Subspace (VR kink), Kink University, Kink.com, Wasteland Weekends, Dark Odyssey
10. What SaaS/platform tech stacks do the biggest kink sites use?

Be thorough. Return a structured document with sections, tables, and data. This will inform a complete upgrade plan.", "context": "This is for upgrading a kink community website in Paraguay called Fun4Me. The client (Rach, from Asuncion) runs a kink/BDSM community. We already have a Next.js 15 + Supabase + Traefik website with ecommerce (54 products), admin panel, auth, blog, events page. We need to upgrade it into a full community platform with events+tickets, education, memberships, subscriptions. The current codebase is at /root/fun4me-store and deploys to fun4me.paragu-ai.com on a Docker Swarm VPS.", "toolsets": ["web", "terminal", "file"]}

function.name: delegate_task
function.arguments:
{"goal": "Do a thorough codebase audit of the Fun4Me store at /root/fun4me-store. I need to understand:

1. Full file tree structure (all directories, components, pages, API routes)
2. Database schema — read all SQL migration files, list EVERY table with columns
3. Current features list: auth, cart, checkout, admin CRUD, blog, events, search, products, coupons
4. API routes: list every endpoint with method and purpose
5. Current payment setup (what's integrated)
6. Email/notification setup
7. All packages/dependencies
8. Existing Supabase schema: what tables, what RLS policies
9. Current event system — how events work, do they have tickets?
10. What's missing for a full community platform: memberships, subscriptions, ticketing, education courses, user profiles, messaging, forums, event calendar, event check-in

Be exhaustive. Read all key files. Return a complete structured audit document.", "context": "The repo is at /root/fun4me-store. It's a Next.js 15 + Supabase + Docker Swarm project for a kink/BDSM community in Paraguay. Built with pnpm. Live at fun4me.paragu-ai.com (not deployed yet). Has .env with Supabase credentials. Use terminal to explore, read_file for key files. Check src/app, src/components, src/lib, content/, database/, etc.", "toolsets": ["terminal", "file"]}

function.name: delegate_task
function.arguments:
{"goal": "Research the kink/BDSM community in Paraguay and Latin America. Find:
1. Any existing kink/BDSM groups, clubs, or communities in Paraguay (FetLife groups, Facebook groups, WhatsApp groups, meetups)
2. FetLife statistics: how many people in Paraguay? What cities? 
3. Existing kink stores in Paraguay/LATAM
4. BDSM event venues in Asuncion and Paraguay
5. Kink-friendly therapists, educators, dominatrices in LATAM
6. Legal landscape: is kink/BDSM content restricted in Paraguay? Payment processor issues?
7. What kink events exist in LATAM (Fetish Week Argentina, etc)
8. Any kink ecommerce stores in LATAM specifically (not just global)
9. Cultural attitudes toward BDSM/kink in Paraguay and Latin America
10. WhatsApp/Telegram groups for kink in LATAM

Return a structured document. The more specific to Paraguay the better.", "context": "This is for Fun4Me, a kink community website based in Asuncion, Paraguay. We need to understand the local market. The target audience is Spanish-speaking kinksters in Paraguay and potentially wider LATAM. Use DuckDuckGo, web search, etc. Try multiple search queries since VPS IPs may get blocked.", "toolsets": ["web", "terminal", "file"]}
